title: Reviving dead dry-erase markers
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"deiod","text":"This is a recipe for reviving dry-erase markers. It is based on the tweet of Scott Barolo and the suggestion to add it to protocols.io is from Justin Kiggins.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":68,"length":5},{"key":1,"offset":143,"length":14}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"5A73E940B27E11E996C6CF6B3EDCDC7B","url":"https://twitter.com/sbarolo/status/1155870573043572738"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"51E9A1C0B27E11E996C6CF6B3EDCDC7B","url":"https://twitter.com/neuromusic/status/1155910220855435264"}}}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"bkfqt","text":"I did get a capful of ink once — be careful out there","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Tape a string to the back of the dry-erase marker as shown below.
Spin the marker like a lasso above your head, about 25 times. (See the video here: https://www.facebook.com/sandy.owens.54/videos/10214113211493293/ .)
There should now be more life in the marker.
creator: Lenny Teytelman
